title: git merge conflict resolution
solution_steps: 1. `git status` to see conflicted files
2. Open files and search for `<<<<<<<`, `=======`, `>>>>>>>` markers
3. Edit to keep the correct changes between markers
4. Remove the conflict markers after resolving
5. `git add <file>` for each resolved file
6. `git commit` to complete the merge
7. Use `git mergetool` for GUI-based resolution
code_example:
git merge feature-branch
# Resolve conflicts
nano src/file.js  # Edit to remove markers
git add src/file.js
git commit -m "Resolve merge conflict"
# Alternative: abort merge
git merge --abort

title: git detached HEAD state recovery
solution_steps: 1. Create a branch at current position: `git branch temp-branch`
2. Switch to the new branch: `git checkout temp-branch`
3. Merge or rebase to integrate with main branch
4. If you just want to go back: `git checkout main`
5. If you made commits you want to keep: `git log` to find commit hash, then `git checkout -b new-branch <hash>`
code_example:
# Save work in detached HEAD
git checkout -b my-temp-branch
# Merge into main
git checkout main
git merge my-temp-branch
# Or simply return to a branch
git checkout main

title: git push rejected (non-fast-forward)
solution_steps: 1. Pull latest changes first: `git pull origin main`
2. Resolve any merge conflicts
3. Try push again: `git push origin main`
4. Use `--force-with-lease` (safer than --force) if you need to overwrite remote
5. Use `git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main` for cleaner history
6. Never `--force` push to shared branches without coordination
code_example:
git pull origin main --rebase
# Resolve conflicts if any
git push origin main
# Force with lease (safer)
git push --force-with-lease origin main

title: git: accidentally committed to wrong branch
solution_steps: 1. Get commit hash: `git log --oneline -1`
2. Switch to correct branch: `git checkout correct-branch`
3. Cherry-pick the commit: `git cherry-pick <hash>`
4. Go back to wrong branch: `git checkout wrong-branch`
5. Reset: `git reset HEAD~1` (soft reset keeps changes staged)
6. Use `git reset --hard HEAD~1` to discard (only if unpushed)
code_example:
git log --oneline -1
# a1b2c3d My commit
git checkout target-branch
git cherry-pick a1b2c3d
git checkout wrong-branch
git reset HEAD~1
git push --force-with-lease origin wrong-branch  # if already pushed

title: git: untracked files preventing branch switch
error_patterns: The following untracked working tree files would be overwritten by checkout
solution_steps: 1. Add and stash: `git add . && git stash`
2. Switch branches: `git checkout other-branch`
3. Restore: `git stash pop` on the new branch
4. Delete untracked files: `git clean -fd` (careful — irreversible)
5. Move files manually to a backup location
code_example:
git add . && git stash -u  # -u includes untracked
git checkout other-branch
git stash pop
# Or force checkout (discards changes)
git checkout -f other-branch

title: git: reset vs revert vs restore
solution_steps: 1. `git reset --soft HEAD~1` — undo commit, keep changes staged
2. `git reset --mixed HEAD~1` — undo commit, keep changes unstaged
3. `git reset --hard HEAD~1` — discard commit AND changes (dangerous)
4. `git revert <hash>` — create new commit that undoes changes (safe for shared history)
5. `git restore <file>` — undo unstaged changes in working directory
6. `git restore --staged <file>` — unstage file
code_example:
# Already pushed (safe):
git revert HEAD  # Creates new commit undoing last
git revert a1b2c3d

# Not pushed:
git reset --soft HEAD~1  # Keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1  # Completely remove commits

title: git: rebase conflicts
solution_steps: 1. Resolve conflicts in each file as they appear
2. `git add <resolved-file>` after fixing
3. `git rebase --continue` to move to next commit
4. Edit commit message if prompted
5. `git rebase --abort` to cancel the entire rebase
6. `git rebase --skip` to skip a problematic commit
7. Use `git mergetool` for visual conflict resolution
code_example:
git rebase main
# Conflict in file.txt
nano file.txt  # Resolve
git add file.txt
git rebase --continue
# Or abort
git rebase --abort

title: git: stash pop conflict
solution_steps: 1. Resolve conflicts as usual (edit conflicted files)
2. `git add <resolved-file>` for each
3. `git stash drop` to remove stash after resolution
4. `git stash apply` (instead of pop) to keep stash for re-try
5. Check `git stash list` to see all stashes
code_example:
git stash pop
# Conflicts occur
nano file.txt  # Resolve
git add file.txt
git stash drop
# Alternative: apply then drop
git stash apply  # Resolve later
git stash drop

title: git: amend and force push
solution_steps: 1. `git commit --amend -m "New message"` (unpushed only)
2. `git commit --amend --no-edit` (keep message, add staged changes)
3. `git push --force-with-lease origin branch` (after amend on pushed branch)
4. Warn collaborators — force push rewrites history
5. Never force push to shared branches (main, develop)
code_example:
git add forgotten-file.js
git commit --amend --no-edit
# If already pushed
git push --force-with-lease origin my-feature
# Change commit message only
git commit --amend -m "New message"

title: git: detached head from checkout of tag/commit
error_patterns: need to create a branch for commits
solution_steps: 1. If you want to make changes: `git checkout -b new-branch-name`
2. If you just want to look: `git checkout main` or original branch
3. Tags are read-only pointers — don't commit on them
4. If you already committed in detached HEAD: create branch to save commits
code_example:
# View tag but not modify
git checkout v1.0
# Okay, done looking, go back
git checkout main

# Want to modify:
git checkout -b hotfix-v1.0 v1.0
# Now make changes, commit, etc.

title: git: large file / binary diff issues
solution_steps: 1. Add large file extensions to `.gitignore`
2. Use `git lfs` (Large File Storage) for binaries
3. Track: `git lfs track "*.psd" "*.zip"`
4. Ensure `.gitattributes` is committed: `git add .gitattributes`
5. Install git-lfs: `git lfs install`
6. Use `--no-renames` for large rename diffs
code_example:
git lfs install
git lfs track "*.psd" "*.zip" "*.bin"
echo ".gitattributes" >> .gitignore
git add .gitattributes

title: git: submodule issues
solution_steps: 1. Clone with submodules: `git clone --recurse-submodules <url>`
2. For existing clone: `git submodule update --init --recursive`
3. Update submodule to latest: `git submodule update --remote`
4. Add a submodule: `git submodule add <url> path/to/submodule`
5. Remove a submodule: deinit then rm
6. Check `.gitmodules` for configuration
code_example:
git clone --recurse-submodules https://github.com/user/repo.git
# Or after clone
git submodule update --init --recursive
# Update all submodules
git submodule foreach git pull origin main

title: git: bisect for finding regression
solution_steps: 1. `git bisect start`
2. Mark current as bad: `git bisect bad`
3. Mark a known good commit: `git bisect good <hash>`
4. Git checks out a midpoint commit
5. Test and mark: `git bisect good` or `git bisect bad`
6. Repeat until the first bad commit is found
7. `git bisect reset` to exit
code_example:
git bisect start
git bisect bad              # Current version is broken
git bisect good v1.0        # v1.0 was working
# Test each midpoint:
git bisect good             # or bad
git bisect reset            # When done

title: git: credential and authentication
solution_steps: 1. Use GitHub CLI: `gh auth login`
2. Use SSH keys: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519`, add to GitHub
3. Use personal access tokens instead of passwords
4. Cache credentials: `git config --global credential.helper osxkeychain` (Mac) or `manager-core` (Windows)
5. Check remote URL: `git remote -v` (HTTPS vs SSH)
6. Update remote: `git remote set-url origin git@github.com:user/repo.git`
code_example:
# Switch to SSH
git remote set-url origin git@github.com:user/repo.git
# Cache credentials (Windows)
git config --global credential.helper manager-core
# Generate SSH key
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "email@example.com"
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub  # Add to GitHub

title: git: squashing multiple commits
solution_steps: 1. `git rebase -i HEAD~<N>` where N is the number of commits to combine
2. Change `pick` to `squash` (or `s`) for all but the first commit
3. Save and exit — Git will combine them
4. Edit the combined commit message
5. `git push --force-with-lease` if already pushed
6. For main branch merges: use `--squash` flag
code_example:
git rebase -i HEAD~3
# Change:
# pick a1b2c3d First commit
# squash b2c3d4e Second commit
# squash c3d4e5f Third commit
# Save and edit commit message
git push --force-with-lease origin feature

title: git: case-sensitive filename issues
solution_steps: 1. Use `git mv -f oldname Newname` for case-only renames
2. Enable: `git config core.ignorecase false`
3. On Windows/Mac, filesystem is case-insensitive by default
4. For two-step rename: `git mv oldname temp && git mv temp Newname`
5. Use `--no-index` for case-sensitive diff
code_example:
git mv Newfile.txt newfile.txt  # Won't work on Windows
# Instead:
git mv Newfile.txt temp_new.txt
git mv temp_new.txt newfile.txt
# Or:
git config core.ignorecase false  # Be careful on Windows
